body:
It is possible to reset Windows Server Remote Desktop Services licensing [120 day] grace period, by removing the registry key  `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\RCM\GracePeriod`
To my surprise, Dell even has an official [article](https://www.dell.com/support/kbdoc/en-us/000193714/how-to-reset-the-windows-remote-desktop-services-licensing-grace-period) on it.
author: DavidGeeraerts

author: okynnor

body: Install the RDP Session Host and RDP Licencing Host roles, then go to the licencing manager, right-click > Activate, fill in junk whenever it asks for any company info, then choose `Select Plus License`, use the key `1234567`, choose the latest product version available, Per Device type, and qty 9999. After that, run gpedit.msc, and go to Computer Config > Admin Templates > Windows Components > Remote Desktop Services > Session Host > Licensing, open `Set the remote desktop licensing mode`, enable it, and choose per-device, then open `Use the specified licensing servers`, enable it, and use `localhost`. Reboot, and Remote Desktop is now fully activated.

body:
> I was Exploring this Topic a little bit when I came to think of which Product key HWID Generator uses to activate windows. Like if it uses the Generic Windows key which are default then doesn't it mean that all the user who is using HWID of MAS is using the same on all the computers? So then it doesn't make it genuine I Guess?

Generic keys come by default with windows so that it can know which edition/channel is installed since it's the key that decides it.

HWID (digital license) activation look for that specific edition/channel, doesn't matter if those keys are generic or not. 

For example - when you upgrade already activated windows 7 to windows 10, it gives you an HWID license on windows 10. Which key do you think is installed at that time? It's a generic key. MAS uses the same kind of generic keys. 

Let me know if there is still any confusion.

body:
Your laptop has an MSDM license in motherboard which is converted to a digital license. 
In this case, it doesn't matter if you install the original key which came with the MSDM license or some generic key.

There is no need to backup any of these two licenses. 
You can extract original key which came with MSDM license with below command and install it.
That way you can have a feeling that you are running original activation.
Open command prompt as admin and enter, it will show the key. Install it in windows settings activation page. 

`wmic path softwarelicensingservice get oa3xoriginalproductkey`

You can not remove HWID activation. Once a system is activated, this activation cannot be removed because the license is stored in the Microsoft servers and not in the user’s system. If you don't want HWID then use KMS38 activation or use the change edition option in extras.

body:
KMS38 is needed to activate Windows Server versions which HWID is unable to activate. 
Online KMS is used for activating Office, Windows 7 and 8. 
It's always recommended you use HWID if it supports your current running windows version.

Yes, you can run HWID a 2nd time if your windows deactivates for any reason, the only times this should happen is when you've changed your hardware or you installed a windows edition that you haven't activated before. 

KMS38 is a weird one, HWID matches the official digital license system and Online KMS matches the official KMS Server activation, both systems are set up by Microsoft and is abused by MAS (Yes, Microsoft does actually provide businesses and such with KMS servers to activate the computers at their workplace or such, more reading on the **official implementation** here: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/tn-archive/ee939272(v=technet.10)?redirectedfrom=MSDN#kms-overview).

However KMS38 is not an officially supported way of activating Windows, essentially it makes Windows think it's activated with a KMS server and the activation will expire in the year 2038 instead of the usual 180 days of real KMS activation.

For any one else that stumbles across this question, here is how you can get Windows 10 Pro for free if you only have a Linux OS available.

1. Download WoeUSB, https://github.com/WoeUSB/WoeUSB/releases
2. Install its dependencies (I only had to install wimtools via `sudo apt install wimtools`)
3. Download the Windows ISO from https://www.microsoft.com/en-ca/software-download/windows10ISO
4. Run WoeUSB against the USB/SSD, for example `sudo ./woeusb-5.2.4.bash --device /path/to/Win10_22H2_English_x64v1.iso /dev/sdX`
    - Note: Set your ISO path and your device.
5. Get a SSD or HDD for the Windows OS and plug it into your computer's SATA port. A USB will not work.
6. Plug in the WoeUSB formatted device.
7. Boot and go through the Windows 10 installation process.

Congrats, you will now have a valid, activated digital license for Windows 10 Pro.

body:
@wstlxx 
To change your Office update channel to Insider, open command prompt as admin and enter

```
reg add HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\ClickToRun\Configuration /v CDNBaseUrl /t REG_SZ /d "https://officecdn.microsoft.com/pr/5440FD1F-7ECB-4221-8110-145EFAA6372F" /f

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\ClickToRun\Configuration /v UnmanagedUpdateURL /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\ClickToRun\Configuration /v UpdateUrl /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\ClickToRun\Configuration /v UpdateToVersion /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\ClickToRun\Updates /v UpdateToVersion /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Office\16.0\Common\OfficeUpdate /v UpdatePath /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Office\16.0\Common\OfficeUpdate /v UpdateBranch /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Office\16.0\Common\OfficeUpdate /v UpdateTargetVersion /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Cloud\Office\16.0\Common\OfficeUpdate /v UpdatePath /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Cloud\Office\16.0\Common\OfficeUpdate /v UpdateBranch /f 2>nul

reg delete HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Cloud\Office\16.0\Common\OfficeUpdate /v UpdateTargetVersion /f 2>nul

"%CommonProgramFiles%\microsoft shared\ClickToRun\OfficeC2RClient.exe" /update user
```

It will upgrade to the Insider build. Login your outlook account in office and it will allow you to use Python in excel for some time, (preview) after that it won't work.

We already tell people to verify the code being ran is in fact unmodified:

"The IRM command in PowerShell downloads a script from a specified URL, and the IEX command executes it."
"Always double-check the URL before executing the command and verify the source if manually downloading files."

author: WindowsAddict

body:
I think they're asking what the remove KMS38 protection option does.

The KMS38 activation is vulnerable to being replaced by a normal 180 day KMS activator, which is why by default it'll "protect" the activation (sets KMS server to localhost & locks the `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SoftwareProtectionPlatform\55c92734-d682-4d71-983e-d6ec3f16059f\XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX\` registry key from edits and modifications).

WindowsAddict is telling you to remove the protection on that key and delete it to rule out any potential corruption in the data stored in said key which could cause random deactivations.

body:
> If I understand correctly, despite the introduction of TSforge, using HWID and Ohook is still the most recommended method for "normal" Win 10/11 and Office.

Yes

> Is TSforge more intended for special cases where HWID doesn’t work or more for the future (e.g. Win 10 ESU...)?

Yes
> Or is it perfectly fine to use it instead of HWID, assuming one can live with the fact that it's not persistent across a Windows reinstallation or feature upgrades?

If you are fine with manually reactivating after reinstall and feature upgrade then it doesn't matter. But HWID is better than TSforge.

> The fact that, unlike Ohook, it doesn’t leave any files in the system to retain the activation status actually sounds pretty good.

The main reason MAS recommends Ohook over TSforge for Office is that Ohook can survive annual feature upgrades, but TSforge can't.
Most people are on Windows 10/11 GAC editions, and it's better if Office activation can survive more than a year.
Also, Ohook can activate O365, which provides the most features.
Needless to say that if you know what you are doing, then it doesn't matter.

> The tables are great, but maybe a short flowchart or a dropdown menu would be quite useful.
What Software/Version do I have -> Solution(s) including pros/cons.

Users are expected to understand the cons and pros themselves basis on the given info. 
Btw, MAS highlights the preferred method in green color depending upon the scenario.

> ESU... it can replace BypassESU from MDL - except for .NET 4 ESU Bypass right?
OCUR...OpenCable Unidirectional Cable Receivers?
RP...Release Preview?
APPXLOB...was this for AppX sideloading on Windows 8/8.1?

Yes. Btw, MAS just does the ESU activation, it doesn't change anything else. BypassESU script can do more, like tweaking the system to get server updates on Windows 8.1, etc.

OCUR, yes

RP is RedPill license, it was in early beta builds of 8 to enable some features. https://archive.org/details/rpfiles-8102-eeap

APPXLOB, yes.

author: WindowsAddict

body:
So Microsoft have [produced a workaround](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/release-health/status-windows-10-22H2#3706msgdesc) for the erroneous 'Out of Support' message which persists even after successfully activating ESU licensing.

It's essentially an installable patch to add a new group policy option, which can then be used to activate a feature override (the 'feature' here, I presume, being the 'Out of Support' message).

However, this is really just an Group Policy deployment method to create the following registry entry:
```
[HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Policies\Microsoft\FeatureManagement\Overrides]
"3108581004"=dword:00000000
```
[I have also attached this as a .reg file (rename from .txt to .reg)](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23347380/Win10.ESU.End.of.Support.Message.Fix.txt)

I have confirmed that adding this registry entry to an ESU enabled machine _without_ installing the Group Policy patch results in the 'Out of Support' message being suppressed.  A restart is still required to activate the rollback however.
